schools.summary: Israel's whoredom with the daughters of Moab at Shittim: 'and Israel abode in Shittim, and the people began to commit whoredom with the daughters of Moab' — encamped at Shittim, the people fell into fornication with the women of Moab (the Israel abode in Shittim, the camp on the edge of the land, the the people began to commit whoredom with the daughters of Moab, the sin of fornication, the snare of Moab, the figure, the snare that the curse could not accomplish, now wrought by seduction, the truth that the enemy who could not curse Israel sought to corrupt them, the counsel of Balaam, Numbers 31:16, to draw them into sin, the figure of the danger that fellowship with the world brings, the whoredom that opened to idolatry, vv.2-3, the snare of fleshly lust, the beginning of the great sin at Peor, 'they joined themselves also unto Baalpeor,' Psalm 106:28); Israel encamped at Shittim (the place of the sin) — the camp (at Shittim); 'the people began to commit whoredom with the daughters of Moab' (the fornication, the snare of Moab, the counsel of Balaam, Numbers 31:16, the figure, the seduction that opened to idolatry, Psalm 106:28, the providence, the whoredom, the idolatry of Peor to follow, vv.2-3) — Henry, Gill, Calvin.

schools.summary: Israel joining itself to Baal-peor and the kindling of God's anger: 'and Israel joined himself unto Baalpeor: and the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel' — the apostasy — 'Israel joined himself unto Baalpeor: and the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel': Israel yoked itself to the idol Baal-peor, and God's anger was kindled against them (the witnesses noting the significance — the apostasy of Israel, joining itself to the false god, the people, drawn by lust into idolatry, yoked to Baal-peor, the shameful idol of Moab, forsaking the living God, the lesson — the heinousness of apostasy and the certainty of God's wrath, the joining to the idol a spiritual adultery, the covenant people cleaving to the false god, 'they joined themselves also unto Baalpeor, and ate the sacrifices of the dead... thus they provoked him to anger,' Psalm 106:28-29, the kindling of God's anger — the holy jealousy of God against the idolatry of his people, the wrath that brought the plague, vv.9, the figure — the warning against apostasy, written for our admonition, 'neither be ye idolaters... neither let us commit fornication, as some of them committed,' 1 Corinthians 10:7-8, the truth that God will not share his glory with idols, his anger kindled against his people's unfaithfulness, the figure of the deadly danger of joining with the world's idols, the wrath of God against apostasy, the plague that followed the sin, the figure of the apostasy that provokes the jealous anger of God); Israel yoking itself to the idol Baal-peor (the apostasy, the spiritual adultery, Psalm 106:28) — the joining (to the idol); 'the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel' (God's anger kindled against the idolatry, the holy jealousy, the wrath that brought the plague, Psalm 106:29; 1 Corinthians 10:7-8, the figure, the warning against apostasy, the providence, the wrath, the command to hang the heads to follow, vv.4-5) — Calvin, Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What does Israel's joining to Baal-peor reveal?

schools.summary: God's commendation of Phinehas's zeal that turned away his wrath: 'Phinehas, the son of Eleazar, the son of Aaron the priest, hath turned my wrath away from the children of Israel, while he was zealous for my sake among them, that I consumed not the children of Israel in my jealousy' — the zeal owned — 'Phinehas... hath turned my wrath away from the children of Israel, while he was zealous for my sake among them, that I consumed not the children of Israel in my jealousy': God declared that Phinehas's zeal had turned away his wrath and spared Israel from being consumed (the witnesses noting the significance — the zeal for God's honour that turned away the wrath, Phinehas, jealous for God's glory with the very jealousy of God, executed judgment and so stayed the consuming anger, the lesson — the value and reward of holy zeal for God, the zeal that is 'for my sake,' not of private passion but of love for God's honour, the jealousy that answers to God's own jealousy against sin, 'the zeal of thine house hath eaten me up,' Psalm 69:9; John 2:17, the turning away of wrath — the one zealous act that spared the nation from destruction, the great commendation — Phinehas's deed 'was counted unto him for righteousness unto all generations for evermore,' Psalm 106:30-31, an act of faith and zeal reckoned as Abraham's faith was reckoned, the figure — the holy zeal that cannot bear God's dishonour, fulfilled supremely in Christ whom the zeal of God's house consumed, John 2:17, the truth that God owns and rewards the zeal that honours him and turns away his wrath, the figure of the faithful servant whose zeal for God's glory is counted for righteousness, the figure of the zeal for God's honour that turns away wrath and is reckoned for righteousness); John 2:17) — the figure (the zeal that honours God, fulfilled in Christ, the providence, the zeal owned, the covenant of peace to follow, vv.12-13) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does God's commendation of Phinehas's zeal establish?

sides.position: The zeal for God's honour, answering to God's own jealousy against sin, that turned away the consuming wrath and spared the nation - an act reckoned for righteousness to all generations (Psalm 106:30-31), a figure of the holy zeal that cannot bear God's dishonour, fulfilled in Christ whom the zeal of God's house consumed (John 2:17; Psalm 69:9)
